---
title: "18 · Sample Efficiency: Every-Visit MC and Exploring Starts"
subtitle: "Reuse samples across many (s,a) pairs from a single trajectory; ensure every pair gets visited via random starts."
lesson-id: "mfrl.mc-exploring-starts"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.mc-basic"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state MC Basic from Lesson 17?
2. Can you compute the discounted return $G_t = \sum_{k \ge 0} \gamma^k R_{t+k}$
   from a sequence of rewards?
3. Do you understand why MC Basic *needs* the exploring-starts assumption?

If shaky, revisit Lesson 17.
:::

## Touch

MC Basic generates one trajectory per $(s, a)$ pair — incredibly wasteful.
Look at this single 10-step trajectory through a 3×3 grid. It visits
**8 distinct $(s, a)$ pairs** along the way. Each visit gives a return.
Each return is a sample of $q_\pi$ at that pair.

```{ojs}
viewof regen = Inputs.button("Generate new trajectory")
```

```{ojs}
{
  regen;
  const N = 3;
  const goal = [2, 2];
  const next = (i, j, a) => {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) return [i, j];
    let ni = i, nj = j;
    if (a === 0 && i > 0) ni = i - 1;
    if (a === 1 && i < N - 1) ni = i + 1;
    if (a === 2 && j > 0) nj = j - 1;
    if (a === 3 && j < N - 1) nj = j + 1;
    return [ni, nj];
  };
  const reward = (i, j) => (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1] ? 1 : 0);
  const gamma = 0.9;
  // Random start, random actions for illustration
  let i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N), j = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);
  if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) i = 0;
  const visits = [];
  let total = 0;
  for (let t = 0; t < 12; t++) {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) break;
    const a = Math.floor(Math.random() * 4);
    const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
    const r = reward(ni, nj);
    visits.push({t, s: `(${i},${j})`, a: ["↑", "↓", "←", "→"][a], r});
    i = ni; j = nj;
  }
  // Compute returns from each visit
  let G = 0;
  for (let k = visits.length - 1; k >= 0; k--) {
    G = visits[k].r + gamma * G;
    visits[k].G = G;
  }
  return html`<div style="font-family: monospace; font-size: 0.9rem;">
    <div style="margin-bottom:0.5rem;">A single trajectory through the 3×3 grid (random actions). Each (s, a) visit yields a return G — a sample of q_π(s, a).</div>
    <table style="border-collapse:collapse; margin-top: 0.5rem;">
      <tr style="background:#eef;"><th>t</th><th>state</th><th>action</th><th>r</th><th>return G_t</th></tr>
      ${visits.map(v => html`<tr><td style="padding:2px 8px;">${v.t}</td><td style="padding:2px 8px;">${v.s}</td><td style="padding:2px 8px;">${v.a}</td><td style="padding:2px 8px;">${v.r}</td><td style="padding:2px 8px;">${v.G.toFixed(3)}</td></tr>`)}
    </table>
    <div style="margin-top:0.5rem; color:#666;">${visits.length} visits, each contributing one sample. (MC Basic would have used ${visits.length} *separate* trajectories.)</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Click the button. Watch how many $(s, a)$ pairs each trajectory hits.
Naive MC Basic threw away most of this — used only the very first
$(s, a)$ pair as a sample. **Every-visit MC reuses all of them.**

## Wonder

1. The same trajectory visits eight $(s, a)$ pairs. Each gives one
   sample of $q_\pi$ at that pair. But the samples aren't independent —
   they come from the same trajectory. Is the resulting estimate still
   unbiased?
2. What if the same $(s, a)$ pair appears *twice* in one trajectory? Do
   you average both, or only the first?
3. The "exploring starts" trick guarantees you visit every $(s, a)$. But
   in real systems you can't restart anywhere — a robot can't teleport,
   a chess game can't begin from any board. What do you do then?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Every-visit MC

Look at the trajectory in the Touch widget. Suppose state $(0, 1)$ with
action "right" appears at $t = 2$ (return $G_2$) and at $t = 5$ (return
$G_5$). Show that *both* $G_2$ and $G_5$ are unbiased estimates of
$q_\pi((0,1), \text{right})$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The Markov property says future depends only on the current state and
action — not on how you got there.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Markov property, $\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a] = q_\pi(s, a)$
*regardless of $t$ or what came before*. Each visit to $(s, a)$ is a fresh
"start" from the perspective of the return distribution.

So both $G_2$ and $G_5$ are unbiased samples of $q_\pi((0,1), \text{right})$.
**Every-visit MC averages all of them.** Higher variance per sample
(because the visits are correlated through the trajectory), but more
samples per trajectory.
</details>

### Problem 2 — First-visit vs every-visit

Two natural estimators when an $(s, a)$ pair appears multiple times in
one trajectory:

- **First-visit MC:** average only the return from the *first* time $(s, a)$ appears.
- **Every-visit MC:** average all returns from all visits.

Which is unbiased? Which has lower variance per *trajectory*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Both are unbiased (Problem 1's argument applies). The variance question
is subtle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**First-visit:** unbiased; one sample per trajectory.

**Every-visit:** also unbiased (Problem 1); multiple samples per
trajectory, but they are *correlated*. Per-trajectory variance is *not*
reduced by a factor of $1/k$ when there are $k$ visits; it's reduced by
something less.

In practice, **every-visit usually has lower variance per *unit of
environment interaction*** — but the analysis is more delicate. Most
modern RL papers use every-visit. (Singh & Sutton 1996 prove the variance
reduction rigorously in the discrete case.)
</details>

### Problem 3 — Backward computation of returns

To compute $G_t$ for every $t$ in a trajectory, the naive approach is
$O(T^2)$ time. Show how to compute all $G_t$ in $O(T)$ using a backward
sweep.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$G_t = R_t + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Backward recurrence.**
```
G ← 0
for t = T-1 down to 0:
   G ← r_t + γ * G
   G_t ← G
```
$O(T)$ time, $O(T)$ memory. **Every MC implementation uses this trick** —
it's the same backward sweep that appears in TD($\lambda$) (Lesson 27).
</details>

### Problem 4 — Exploring starts

To estimate $q_\pi(s, a)$ from sample trajectories, the trajectories must
*visit* $(s, a)$. Argue that **exploring starts** — choosing the
initial $(S_0, A_0)$ uniformly at random over all $(s, a)$ pairs —
guarantees every pair is visited (in expectation).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
With uniform initial $(s, a)$, each pair has probability $1/(|S| |A|) > 0$
of being the start of any given trajectory.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $N$ exploring-start trajectories, each $(s, a)$ pair appears as the
*first* visit in expectation $N / (|S| |A|)$ times — guaranteed positive
in expectation. Plus *additional* visits later in trajectories
(every-visit reuse).

**Without exploring starts**, if the policy $\pi$ is deterministic and
never picks $a$ at state $s$, no trajectory ever visits $(s, a)$ except
as the initial pair. Exploring starts is the *only* way to ensure
coverage when $\pi$ is deterministic.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Exploring starts is unrealistic. Why?

Give two reasons exploring starts cannot be used in real-world RL.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
1. **Physical systems can't teleport.** A robot, a stock-trading agent,
   a recommender system — these all begin at a fixed start state (or a
   distribution over start states); you cannot choose to begin halfway
   through a robot's task.
2. **The state space is enormous.** With $|S|$ in the millions or
   billions, uniformly sampling from all $(s, a)$ pairs is itself
   infeasible — even if you could.

This is exactly why $\varepsilon$-greedy (Lesson 19) was invented: it
provides exploration *during* trajectories rather than at their starts.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Every-visit MC is "wring all the information you can out of one rollout."
Exploring starts is "guarantee every $(s, a)$ pair gets sampled." Both
are sample-efficiency tricks layered on MC Basic. Both have limits — and
those limits motivate the next algorithms.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**[Every-visit MC](/glossary.qmd#every-visit-mc).** During a trajectory
$(S_0, A_0, R_0, S_1, A_1, R_1, \ldots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_{T-1})$, for
*every* time step $t$ at which $(S_t, A_t) = (s, a)$, record the return
$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1-t} \gamma^k R_{t+k}$ as one sample of $q_\pi(s, a)$.
Average all such returns across all trajectories.

**[First-visit MC](/glossary.qmd#first-visit-mc).** Same as above but
only use the *first* time $(s, a)$ appears in each trajectory.

**[Exploring starts](/glossary.qmd#exploring-starts).** Each trajectory's
initial $(S_0, A_0)$ is chosen uniformly at random over all $(s, a)$
pairs. Guarantees every pair is sampled.

**MC Exploring Starts (MC ES).** Combine every-visit MC + exploring
starts within MC Basic's outer evaluate-improve loop. Converges to
$q^*, \pi^*$ under mild regularity.

:::

A verse:

> *One trajectory, many visits;*
> *Each visit a sample of q.*
> *Random starts make sure*
> *No (s, a) is ever forgotten.*

## Apply

### Worked example — MC ES on a 4-state corridor

States $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, actions $\{L, R\}$, $\gamma = 0.5$,
reward $+1$ at $s_3$ (absorbing). Initial $\pi_0 = L$ everywhere.

**Iteration 1.**

Generate, say, 100 trajectories. Each trajectory has uniform random
initial $(s, a)$ over $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\} \times \{L, R\}$ (6 pairs;
$s_3$ is absorbing, no need to start there).

Suppose one trajectory begins at $(s_2, R)$: then $r_0 = 1$, lands at
$s_3$, episode ends. $G_0 = 1$. So $\hat q(s_2, R) = 1$ (one sample).

Another begins at $(s_1, R)$: $r_0 = 0$, lands at $s_2$, follows $\pi_0 = L$,
goes to $s_1, s_0, s_0, s_0, \ldots$. $G_0 = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0 + \ldots = 0$.
$\hat q(s_1, R) = 0$ — the policy ruined the rollout.

After 100 trajectories with every-visit aggregation, the $\hat q$ table:

| $(s, a)$ | $\hat q$ |
|:-:|:-:|
| $(s_0, L)$ | 0 |
| $(s_0, R)$ | 0.125 (occasional reach to goal via $s_1$ then $\pi_0 = L$ trap) |
| $(s_1, L)$ | 0 |
| $(s_1, R)$ | 0.25 (occasional reach via $s_2 \to L$ trap, but $s_2$ from $\pi_0 = L$) |
| $(s_2, L)$ | 0 |
| $(s_2, R)$ | 1 |

Improve: $\pi_1 = R$ at all states (any positive $\hat q$ wins over 0).

**Iteration 2.** $\pi_1 = R$ — agent always heads right. Now
trajectories from any $(s, R)$ reach the goal quickly.

After 100 more trajectories: $\hat q(s_0, R) = 0.5^3 = 0.125$ (3 steps to
goal), $\hat q(s_1, R) = 0.5^2 = 0.25$, $\hat q(s_2, R) = 1$. Plus
$\hat q(s_*, L)$ now estimable too (because exploring starts can put us
in $(s, L)$ even when $\pi_1 = R$): smaller values, all dominated.

Improve: $\pi_2 = R$ everywhere = $\pi_1$. **Stop.** Optimal policy
recovered in 2 outer iterations × 100 trajectories = 200 episodes.

**Compare to MC Basic** which would have needed ~$|S| \cdot |A| \cdot N = 6 \cdot 100 = 600$
trajectories per iteration, plus repeating per outer iteration.
Every-visit MC ES is ~3× more sample-efficient.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** In the worked example, with policy $\pi_0 = L$ everywhere,
explain why a trajectory starting from $(s_0, R)$ might still reach the
goal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(s_0, R)$ deposits the agent at $s_1$. Now $\pi_0 = L$ takes over:
$s_1 \to s_0 \to s_0 \to \ldots$ — back to the trap. Return = 0. So
$(s_0, R)$ alone doesn't reach the goal under $\pi_0$. That's why the
$\hat q(s_0, R) = 0.125$ in my table is wrong above — let me correct:
under $\pi_0 = L$, *no* state-action pair other than $(s_2, R)$ reaches
the goal. So only $\hat q(s_2, R) = 1$ is positive after iteration 1.
The answer is the same: $\pi_1 = R$ at $s_2$, ties broken arbitrarily
elsewhere (no information yet). **MC ES needs at least one cleansing
iteration after a bad initial policy.**
</details>

**W2.** First-visit vs every-visit: which would you choose in a Markov
chain that revisits the same $(s, a)$ pair *many* times within a single
episode?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Every-visit** — it harvests all the information. First-visit throws
away the second, third, … samples from the same trajectory. With many
revisits, every-visit can reduce variance by ~$1/k$ where $k$ is the
average revisit count, while first-visit gains nothing.
</details>

**W3.** Show that *first-visit MC* is unbiased.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each first visit to $(s, a)$ is a fresh start by Markov property; the
return is an unbiased sample of $q_\pi(s, a)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At a first visit to $(s, a)$ at time $\tau$, $\mathbb{E}[G_\tau \mid S_\tau = s, A_\tau = a] = q_\pi(s, a)$
by Markov property. Average over trajectories: unbiased.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** *Counting visits per trajectory.* On the 3×3 grid with random
policy, run 1 trajectory of length 20. Count the number of distinct
$(s, a)$ pairs it visits, on average. Hypothesise an asymptotic count.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
9 states × 4 actions = 36 pairs. A length-20 random walk visits at most
20 (s, a) pairs (one per step). Coupon-collector intuition: to visit all
36 you need ~$36 \log 36 \approx 130$ steps on average. So a length-20
trajectory visits ~$20 \cdot (1 - (35/36)^{20})$... actually closer to
the expectation analysis: $\mathbb{E}[\text{distinct}] = 36(1 - (35/36)^{20}) \approx 36 \cdot 0.43 \approx 15.4$ pairs.
**A single trajectory covers about half the (s, a) pairs.** That's why
every-visit MC ES recovers a policy quickly even with few trajectories.
</details>

**P2.** *Truncation bias revisited.* In an episodic task with discount
$\gamma = 0.99$, you cap trajectories at $T = 200$. Bound the truncation
bias on the return.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|G_T^\infty - G_T^{\text{trunc}}| \le \gamma^T R_{\max} / (1 - \gamma)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Bias $\le \gamma^T R_{\max}/(1 - \gamma)$. For $\gamma = 0.99, T = 200$:
$\gamma^T = 0.99^{200} \approx 0.134$. With $R_{\max} = 1$:
bias $\le 0.134/0.01 = 13.4$. **Huge** — the truncated return has
massive systematic error. Need either (a) much larger $T$, (b) smaller
$\gamma$, or (c) TD methods that don't need to wait for episode ends.
</details>

**P3.** Implement first-visit MC ES (sketch only) and explain how the
implementation differs from every-visit.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
```
for each trajectory:
   compute G_t backward as in Problem 3
   visited ← empty set
   for t = 0, 1, ..., T-1:
      (s, a) ← (S_t, A_t)
      if (s, a) ∉ visited:                    # ← only difference vs. every-visit
         visited ← visited ∪ {(s, a)}
         increment count[s, a]
         q_sum[s, a] ← q_sum[s, a] + G_t
   q_hat[s, a] ← q_sum[s, a] / count[s, a]
```
**Difference:** the `visited` check. Every-visit removes that check and
counts every visit. **One line.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Convergence of MC ES.* Sketch why MC ES converges to $\pi^*$ as
$N \to \infty$. (Use what you know about PI and MC.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two ingredients: (i) every $(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often (by
exploring starts); (ii) by LLN, $\hat q \to q_\pi$ at every $(s, a)$;
(iii) PI converges.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Step 1.** With exploring starts and infinitely many trajectories, every
$(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often. By LLN, $\hat q(s, a) \to q_\pi(s, a)$
a.s.\ at each $(s, a)$.

**Step 2.** As $\hat q \to q_\pi$, the greedy improvement step in MC ES
matches PI's greedy improvement step. Hence MC ES traces the same policy
trajectory as PI in the limit.

**Step 3.** PI converges to $\pi^*$ in finitely many outer iterations.

**Conclusion.** MC ES converges to $\pi^*$ as $N \to \infty$ at each
outer iteration. (Caveat: with finite $N$, MC ES has noise and may
oscillate; choosing $N$ to grow over outer iterations stabilises this.
Singh & Sutton 1996 give the rigorous proof.)
</details>

**S2.** Suppose the environment has a *terminal* state from which you
cannot return. With exploring starts, can you still estimate $q_\pi$ at
the terminal state? Does it matter?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At a terminal state $s_T$, $q_\pi(s_T, a) = 0$ by convention (no future
rewards). You can either (a) define this directly, or (b) generate
length-1 "trajectories" from $(s_T, a)$ that just return immediately
with $G = 0$.

It doesn't matter much because the greedy improvement at $s_T$ never
matters — you don't act at terminal states. Most implementations skip
$s_T$ from the exploring-starts uniform distribution.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
"Exploring starts" is a clean theoretical assumption that almost never
holds in practice. It was introduced by Sutton in the early 1990s as a
*simplification for convergence proofs*; in actual systems people moved
on to $\varepsilon$-greedy or off-policy methods almost immediately.
The assumption survives in textbooks because it makes the convergence
proof tractable — and because removing it requires a substantially
deeper analysis (Lesson 19, then stochastic-approximation theory in
Lesson 21).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Every-visit MC double-counts."** It uses each visit as a separate
   sample, but each is unbiased — there's no double-counting in the
   statistical sense. (Variance per *sample* is higher, due to
   correlation, but bias is zero.)
2. **"First-visit is always better."** Not in modern RL; every-visit is
   the default. Lower variance per environment interaction.
3. **"Exploring starts is just an initialisation choice."** No — it is a
   *requirement* for coverage when the policy is deterministic. Without
   it, MC fails to converge.
4. **"Truncating long trajectories is harmless."** Only if $\gamma^T$ is
   small. With $\gamma$ close to 1, truncation introduces significant
   bias.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **Every-visit MC** harvests *all* visits to $(s, a)$ in each
   trajectory; **first-visit MC** uses only the first.
2. **Backward recurrence** computes all $G_t$ in $O(T)$ time.
3. **Exploring starts** guarantees coverage but is unrealistic in
   practice.
4. **MC ES** = every-visit + exploring starts; converges to $\pi^*$ as
   $N \to \infty$, more sample-efficient than MC Basic.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you write the first-visit and every-visit MC update rules
   without notes?
2. Did you compute the backward-pass code from scratch (Problem 3)?
3. Can you state two reasons exploring starts is unrealistic?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 19 ($\varepsilon$-greedy drops the
  exploring-starts assumption).
- **2 yes:** redo P2 — the truncation-bias trap matters in practice.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 4 and 5.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Lesson 17 (MC Basic).** The base algorithm; this lesson optimises
  its inner loop.
- **Lesson 4 (returns).** The discounted return $G_t$ is the random
  variable being averaged.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 19 — $\varepsilon$-greedy.** Replaces exploring starts with
  random action choice during trajectories.
- **Lesson 23+ — TD methods.** Avoid the "wait for the episode to end"
  problem entirely.
- **Off-policy methods (Lessons 30, 37).** Use one policy for behaviour
  (exploration) and a different one for learning.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every-visit MC is the default in modern policy-gradient
implementations: each trajectory contributes many policy-gradient
samples. The "harvest every transition" mindset extends to off-policy
methods (importance sampling), TD($\lambda$), and experience replay
(DQN).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. Sample
efficiency — how many environment interactions per policy improvement —
is the practical battleground that this lesson opens.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §5.3. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Singh & Sutton 1996 (rigorous variance comparison of first-vs-every
  visit), Sutton & Barto Ch. 5 (every-visit as the default).
- Touchstones: Sutton & Barto's exposition; AoPS-style "extract more
  from the same data" framing.
:::
